notes.note: Pansa's father-in-law is not named in the letter; he was a figure associated with the Antonian faction whom Pansa nonetheless attacked in the Senate, demonstrating his public commitment to the republican cause over family loyalty.

notes.anchor: a consul to whom from the start spirit was not lacking, nor at the end fidelity
notes.note: Pansa (cos. 43) was wounded at the battle of Forum Gallorum (April 43 BC) and died shortly after; Cicero's phrase is a carefully worded epitaph for a man whose political courage he here endorses.

"Brutus" here is Decimus Iunius Brutus Albinus (not Marcus), who commanded the relieved force at Mutina; Cicero criticizes Decimus for failing to pursue Antony's retreating and disorganized army after the battles of Forum Gallorum and Mutina.

M. Aemilius Lepidus, governor of Narbonese Gaul and Nearer Spain, had long been suspected of sympathy with Antony; the delay after Mutina allowed Antony to reach Lepidus, who joined him in May 43 BC, forming the nucleus of the future triumvirate.

notes.anchor: a most settled hope of the consulship
notes.note: Octavian (the young Caesar) was pressing for a consulship despite being only nineteen — far below the legal minimum age; Cicero here acknowledges he had been outmanoeuvred, as Octavian secured the consulship in August 43 BC.

Velia (modern Castellammare di Bruca, Campania) was a coastal town where Cicero met Brutus in August 44 BC as Cicero was about to sail for Greece; Brutus persuaded him to turn back and resume the fight at Rome.
Cicero refers to his Philippic orations, the series of speeches delivered from September 44 BC onward attacking Antony; the First Philippic was delivered in the Senate on 2 September 44 BC.
